concept_ref: §2 Level 4a — 'All 2^N combinations' / §3 row 4 'BCC and MCDC are the same technique'

scenario.title: Triple Lock — Launch Sequence
scenario.narrative: A launch sequence fires only when all three sensor checks pass. The QA engineer submits five tests and claims Branch Condition Combination coverage. How many of the 2^3 = 8 combinations are actually exercised?

claim: Which is the STRONGEST coverage level the test set below actually satisfies?
charges: The defendant function launch(a, b, c) fires the launch sequence only when all three sensor checks are simultaneously true.
The QA team submits five tests — TTT, FFF, TFF, FTF, FFT — and claims the suite satisfies Branch Condition Combination coverage of `a and b and c`.
The prosecution alleges that three of the eight required combinations (TTF, TFT, FTT) are missing from the suite, leaving interaction faults among the conditions undetectable.

options.label: Statement only
options.explanation: Too weak. Both branches of the if execute (T1 fires launch_now, T2-T5 fall through), so Decision and Branch coverage are also met.

options.label: Decision only
options.explanation: Decision is satisfied (TRUE outcome via T1, FALSE outcome via T2-T5), but BC is also satisfied — every condition is observed both T and F. So Decision is not the STRONGEST.

options.label: Branch Condition (each condition T and F)
options.explanation: Correct. a: T (T1, T3) and F (T2, T4, T5). b: T (T1, T4) and F (T2, T3, T5). c: T (T1, T5) and F (T2, T3, T4). All three observed T and F → BC ✓. BCC requires ALL 2^3=8 combinations; only 5 of 8 are present (TTT, FFF, TFF, FTF, FFT). Missing: TTF, TFT, FTT.

options.label: BCC — all 2^N combinations covered
options.explanation: TRAP. BCC requires every single one of 2^3 = 8 input combinations. Only 5 are present (TTT, FFF, TFF, FTF, FFT). Missing: TTF, TFT, FTT — three rows. An interaction bug between conditions could hide there.

correct_answer_explanation: BC is satisfied (each condition observed T and F). BCC is NOT — only 5 of 8 combinations are tested, so an interaction bug in TTF, TFT, or FTT could escape.
wrong_answer_explanation: BCC requires all 2^N combinations. Count the rows present and compare against 2^3 = 8.
hints: BCC requires every one of 2^N input combinations to be tested. With N=3 conditions, that means 8 distinct rows of the truth table.
Tally the present combinations: TTT, FFF, TFF, FTF, FFT — that is 5 unique rows out of 8. Which 3 rows are missing? (TTF, TFT, FTT)
BC is satisfied (each of a, b, c is observed T and F). But BCC is NOT: only 5 of 8 combinations are tested. The 3 missing rows can hide an interaction bug. Strongest satisfied = BRANCH CONDITION.

seeded_faults.description: An operator typo in production reads `a and b or c` instead of `a and b and c`. This bug is invisible to BC but caught by BCC at row TTF.

misconceptions.explanation_md: BCC and MCDC are NOT the same. BCC demands all 2^N combinations (here, 8 tests). MCDC demands only N+1 carefully engineered independence pairs (here, 4 tests). BCC is exhaustive but exponentially expensive; MCDC is targeted but conceptually harder. The next case will show the cost gap explicitly.
